summary:
The Qing Literary Inquisition (c. 1660s–1780s, with peak under the [[event:2463|Qianlong Emperor]]) was a series of campaigns by the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]] to suppress texts deemed disloyal, anti-Manchu, or culturally subversive — a systematic effort by the Manchu ruling house to control Chinese intellectual life and prevent the expression of Han Chinese nationalism or criticism of Qing rule. The inquisition involved the compilation of the Siku Quanshu (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries), a massive library project begun in 1773 that required the submission of private collections across the empire — giving the court the opportunity to identify and destroy texts deemed problematic while preserving others. An estimated 2,300 titles were completely destroyed and 400 more were substantially altered.

The literary inquisition targeted several categories of dangerous material: texts criticizing the Manchu as 'barbarians' or expressing nostalgia for the Ming dynasty, historical works depicting Manchu as tributaries or enemies of earlier Chinese dynasties, and private correspondence that touched on sensitive political questions. Authors and publishers of condemned texts faced severe punishments including execution; in some cases, the families of deceased authors were punished for texts written generations earlier. The inquisition created a pervasive atmosphere of intellectual self-censorship among Chinese scholars — many of the most gifted minds retreated into politically safe scholarship (phonology, textual criticism, evidential scholarship) rather than risking politically sensitive work. This withdrawal of intellectual talent from political and philosophical writing may have contributed to the intellectual stagnation that left China poorly equipped to respond to the challenges of the 19th century.
